coredata.dc:title: Experimental investigation on flow and heat transfer characteristics of eutectic Sn-Bi alloy in a circular tube
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yuqing
Sang, Guanghao
Li, Nan
Wang, Lei
Tang, Shousheng
Deng, Zhongshan
coredata.dc:description: Compared with conventional liquid metals such as sodium (Na) and lead–bismuth eutectic (LBE), eutectic tin–bismuth alloy (ESnBi) exhibits notable advantages including higher volumetric heat capacity, moderate thermal conductivity, lead-free with lower toxicity, and chemical inertness in air, making it a promising candidate for medium-to-high temperature heat transfer applications (T > 150 °C). However, studies on its thermal hydraulic behavior remain scarce. To address this research gap, the present work experimentally investigates the flow and heat transfer characteristics of ESnBi under turbulent flow conditions in a circular tube with an inner diameter of 14 mm. Experiments were conducted under uniform wall heat fluxes ranging from 12.2 kW/m2 to 83.6 kW/m2, corresponding to Reynolds numbers (Re) between 5,000 and 25,000 and Peclet numbers (Pe) between 200 and 1,000. The convective heat transfer coefficient and pressure drop were measured, and empirical correlations were developed. The proposed Nusselt number (Nu) correlation, Nu = 4.47 + 0.016Pe 0.78, exhibits close agreement with established correlations for LBE and demonstrates strong predictive capability across heavy liquid metals, as confirmed by relative standard deviation (RSD) analysis. Additionally, a frictional resistance coefficient (f) correlation for the hydraulically smooth regime was established: f = 0.3716/Re 0.21, which is approximately 1.7 times greater than the classical Blasius equation, indicating flow resistance behavior similar to that of LBE in the transitionally rough regime. This deviation may result from oxide inclusions and corrosion effects within ESnBi, which merit further investigation. Overall, this study provides essential experimental data to support the engineering application and thermal system design of ESnBi as a promising heat transfer fluid.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Eutectic tin–bismuth alloy
Convective heat transfer
Circular tube
Nusselt number
Frictional resistance coefficient

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the experimental facility; (b) Test section details; (c) Photograph of the experimental facility. (1- Electromagnetic pump, 2- Pressure sensor, 3- Thermocouple, 4- Flowmeter, 5- Expansion tank, 6- Liquid level probe, 7- Test section, 8- Heat exchanger, 9- Electromagnetic pump control system, 10- Valve, 11- ESnBi storage and melting tank, 12- Ar gas cylinder).
Illustration of the uncertainty contributions of the input quantities on (a) Nu, and (b) f.
(a) Variation of h with Re; (b) Relationship between Nu and Pe.
Comparison between the proposed Nu-Pe correlation and previously reported correlations for (a) liquid metals, (b) LBE and Hg, (c) Na and NaK, and (d) GaInSn.
Comparison of experimental data for ESnBi with reported LBE data from the literature.

Comparison of experimental data with Nu values calculated fromcorrelations by (a) Eq. (21), (b) Cheng and Tak [31], (c) Ibragimov et al. [27], (d) Zhang et al. [29], (e) Liu et al. [30], (f) Subbotin et al. [25], (g) Skupinski et al. [26], (h) Laube et al. [14].
Comparison of the proposed f-Re correlation with previously reported correlations and the Blasius equation.
Comparison of Na, LBE, and ESnBi (450 K).

Review of heat transfer correlations for liquid metal flows under uniform heat flux conditions.
Relative standard deviations between various heat transfer correlations and the experimental data.
Summary of reported frictional factor correlations for liquid metal flow in pipes.

head.ce:title: Experimental investigation on flow and heat transfer characteristics of eutectic Sn-Bi alloy in a circular tube

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Cryogenic Science and Technology
Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Compared with conventional liquid metals such as sodium (Na) and lead–bismuth eutectic (LBE), eutectic tin–bismuth alloy (ESnBi) exhibits notable advantages including higher volumetric heat capacity, moderate thermal conductivity, lead-free with lower toxicity, and chemical inertness in air, making it a promising candidate for medium-to-high temperature heat transfer applications (T > 150 °C). However, studies on its thermal hydraulic behavior remain scarce. To address this research gap, the present work experimentally investigates the flow and heat transfer characteristics of ESnBi under turbulent flow conditions in a circular tube with an inner diameter of 14 mm. Experiments were conducted under uniform wall heat fluxes ranging from 12.2 kW/m2 to 83.6 kW/m2, corresponding to Reynolds numbers (Re) between 5,000 and 25,000 and Peclet numbers (Pe) between 200 and 1,000. The convective heat transfer coefficient and pressure drop were measured, and empirical correlations were developed. The proposed Nusselt number (Nu) correlation, Nu = 4.47 + 0.016Pe 0.78, exhibits close agreement with established correlations for LBE and demonstrates strong predictive capability across heavy liquid metals, as confirmed by relative standard deviation (RSD) analysis. Additionally, a frictional resistance coefficient (f) correlation for the hydraulically smooth regime was established: f = 0.3716/Re 0.21, which is approximately 1.7 times greater than the classical Blasius equation, indicating flow resistance behavior similar to that of LBE in the transitionally rough regime. This deviation may result from oxide inclusions and corrosion effects within ESnBi, which merit further investigation. Overall, this study provides essential experimental data to support the engineering application and thermal system design of ESnBi as a promising heat transfer fluid.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: distance from thermocouple to inner wall (m)

ce:section.ce:para: Liquid metals have garnered considerable attention as promising heat transfer media owing to their exceptional thermophysical properties, including superior thermal conductivity (typically 8–90 W/(m·K)) [1], excellent fluidity (low dynamic viscosity, order 10−4–10−3 Pa·s) [2], low vapor pressure (e.g. Ga ≈ 1 Pa at ∼1000 °C; LBE ≈ 1 Pa at ∼700 °C; Na ≈ 1 Pa at ∼280 °C) [3–5], and wide liquid-phase temperature ranges (from room temperature to more than 2000 °C) [6]. These advantages make liquid metals particularly suitable for applications involving limited heat transfer areas and extremely high heat flux conditions [7]. Currently, liquid metals have been widely adopted in various certain important technological applications, such as nuclear reactors [8–10], aerospace [11–13], solar power generation [14–16], and thermal management of high-power electronic devices [17–19]. The expanding deployment of liquid metals in these frontier applications has necessitated an in-depth understanding of their fundamental thermal-hydraulic behaviors.

Although water and water-based nanofluids are also frequently employed in a broad range of thermofluidic systems owing to their technological maturity, their relatively low thermal conductivity (about 0.6–2 W/(m·K)) fundamentally limits the attainable heat transfer performance, generally necessitating high pumping power to remove large heat fluxes [20,21]. In addition, nanofluid suspensions introduce further concerns, including erosion, corrosion, and particle sedimentation [22]. In contrast, liquid metals typically exhibit thermal conductivities that are approximately two orders of magnitude higher and therefore emerge as highly suitable alternative coolants for extreme operating conditions. Meanwhile, their Prandtl numbers are usually below 0.05, whereas those of conventional fluids are typically above 0.6. As a result, the thermal boundary layer in liquid metals is much thinner than the momentum boundary layer, leading to significant differences in convective heat transfer mechanisms. These unique characteristics require the development of dedicated theoretical models and experimental investigations.

Driven by the demands of the nuclear industry, studies on liquid metal flow and heat transfer characteristics in pipes have steadily advanced. Martinelli [23] was the first to propose a convective heat transfer model for liquid metal flow in circular tubes, introducing an analogy between turbulent momentum and energy transfer while incorporating the effect of molecular conduction. Building on this, Lyon [24] established an empirical correlation under constant wall heat flux conditions, though later studies found it significantly overestimated heat transfer coefficients, rendering it an upper theoretical bound.

Subsequently, extensive experimental investigations have been conducted on forced convection of liquid metals in circular tubes. Subbotin et al. [25] investigated the heat transfer behavior of sodium (Na) in four types of tubes under transitional and turbulent regimes and proposed a new empirical correlation. Skupinski et al. [26] measured a convective heat transfer coefficient of NaK in a horizontal circular tube and developed correlations over a broad Peclet number range. Ibragimov et al. [27] examined the turbulent heat transfer performance of heavy liquid metals such as mercury, lead, and lead–bismuth eutectic (LBE), highlighting the critical influence of oxides and thermal contact resistance. Laube et al. [28] analyzed the effects of azimuthally non-uniform wall heat flux in turbulent liquid metal flows, revealing that asymmetric heating significantly alters local Nusselt numbers and flow structures. Zhang et al. [29] systematically evaluated the forced convective heat transfer and flow resistance characteristics of LBE in a vertical circular tube and conducted a comparative analysis with Na. Liu et al. [30] experimentally investigated the effect of pipe inclination on the convective heat transfer performance of LBE, showing enhanced local heat transfer intensity compared to vertical pipes. Cheng and Tak [31] provided a comprehensive review of turbulent heat transfer correlations for LBE in a circular tube and proposed models applicable across a wide Peclet number range.

To date, substantial progress has been made in understanding the thermal-hydraulic behavior of Na, NaK, and LBE in circular tubes. However, the high chemical reactivity of Na and the toxicity of LBE pose significant limitations for their broader deployment in industrial applications. In parallel, attention has turned to low melting point liquid metals, such as gallium (Ga) and gallium–indium–tin alloys (GaInSn), which can operate at low-to-medium temperatures and exhibit excellent heat transfer capability. However, the use of Ga-based alloys in large-scale circulation loops under medium-to-high temperature operating conditions faces practical barriers, including high material cost [32], strong wetting, and potential liquid metal embrittlement of structural metals [33]. In contrast, eutectic tin–bismuth alloy (ESnBi, 42 wt.% Sn and 58 wt.% Bi) is a promising alternative due to its relatively low melting point (∼138 °C), moderate thermophysical properties, and superior safety profile (Table 1 ), making it attractive for medium-to-high temperature thermal systems [34]. Although empirical correlations developed for Na, NaK, and LBE may offer preliminary guidance for engineering calculations, differences in thermophysical properties and interactions with structural materials mean that ESnBi cannot be presumed to follow the same thermal hydraulic behavior. As highlighted by Kirillov [35], considerable discrepancies exist among the experimental data of different liquid metals, further emphasizing the necessity for fluid-specific experimental studies. Nevertheless, research on the flow and heat transfer characteristics of ESnBi remains virtually unexplored.

In light of the above context, this study systematically investigates the flow and heat transfer behavior of ESnBi in a circular tube. New empirical correlations for convective heat transfer and frictional resistance are developed, and the experimental results are compared with existing data for other liquid metals. Finally, the limitations of the present work are identified, and potential directions for future investigation are proposed.

An experimental facility was designed and constructed to systematically investigate the thermal-hydraulic characteristics of ESnBi within a circular tube. All experiments were conducted under turbulent flow conditions (Re > 4000). The thermophysical properties of ESnBi at 450 K, which was chosen as a representative bulk temperature for the experimental operating conditions, along with their associated uncertainties, are summarized in Table 2 . Since no uncertainty is reported for the specific heat capacity at constant pressure in Ref. [39], a 5 % uncertainty was assigned, consistent with typical estimates for the constant pressure specific heat of liquid metals [14].

It should be noted that Ref. [38] reports a ESnBi with a composition of 43 wt.% Sn and 57 wt.% Bi, whereas Ref. [39] adopts 42 wt.% Sn and 58 wt.% Bi. Both compositions are commonly referred to as eutectic tin–bismuth alloy in the literature, and such small differences in nominal composition are frequently reported in experimental studies on ESnBi [40,41]. Although the present experiments were performed with an alloy of 42 wt.% Sn and 58 wt.% Bi, the thermophysical property data for ESnBi provided in Ref. [38] are also used as a reference in this work. The ±1 wt.% discrepancy in composition is within typical manufacturing tolerances and is not expected to introduce a significant deviation in the relevant thermophysical properties.

Fig. 1(a) presents a schematic of the experimental loop, which primarily consists of an electromagnetic pump, a mass flowmeter, a test section, and a heat exchanger. An annular linear induction electromagnetic pump was employed as the driving unit, owing to its excellent sealing performance and stable operational characteristics. For flow rate measurement, Coriolis mass flowmeters have been widely employed in liquid metal test loops using LBE, Na, and GaInSn as working fluids and are commonly used as reference instruments to calibrate electromagnetic flowmeters [28,42–45]. Moreover, previous studies have demonstrated that Coriolis mass flowmeters can achieve lower measurement uncertainty than electromagnetic flowmeters for GaInSn [28]. Therefore, a Coriolis mass flowmeter (FTM-1600A, Doweston) was selected in the present study to measure the flow rate of ESnBi.

The test section comprised a horizontal straight tube with an inner diameter of 14 mm, a wall thickness of 3 mm, and a total length of 1 m, as illustrated in Fig. 1(b). Six wall temperature measurement positions (Tow-1–Tow-6) were set along the axial direction at 200 mm intervals. At each location, four K-type armored thermocouples (1 mm in diameter) were mounted at 90° intervals around the tube circumference to minimize measurement errors. All thermocouples were embedded in pre-machined grooves within the tube wall, with the thermocouple junctions positioned 2.5 mm (δ) from the inner wall surface. In addition, two K-type armored thermocouples (Tlm, in and Tlm, out) were set at the center of the tube at both inlet and outlet of the test section to measure the fluid temperature of the ESnBi. The K-type thermocouples were enclosed in 316L stainless steel sheaths. During the entire experimental test process, no failures or temperature drift were observed. Therefore, the influence of ESnBi on the thermocouples (e.g., corrosion or mechanical wear of the sheath) can be regarded as negligible. Two pressure sensors were installed 300 mm upstream and downstream of the test section to capture the pressure drop across the segment.

The entire experimental loop was wrapped with heating wires. The heating wires on the test section were independently controlled to adjust the wall heat flux, while those on the remaining sections were used for preheating and maintaining the temperature of ESnBi within the tubes. A thermal insulation layer over 10 cm thick was applied to minimize heat loss from the experimental system. The test section was fabricated from precision 316L stainless steel, while the remaining pipeline was constructed using standard #20 carbon steel. Additional thermocouples were affixed to critical locations across the experimental loop to monitor temperature distribution in real time and prevent local overheating. A photograph of the experimental facility is presented in Fig. 1(c). The geometric parameters and operating conditions of the experimental facility are listed in Table 3 .

Prior to each experimental run, the ESnBi was first melted in a storage tank, while the entire experimental loop was simultaneously preheated. High-purity argon gas (99.999 %) was then used to purge the entire loop, and this purge-vent cycle was repeated three times to remove residual air and moisture. After purging, high-purity argon gas was introduced into the storage tank to pressurize and drive the molten metal into the loop. The filling process was monitored via a top-mounted level gauge, and once the loop was fully filled, the valve between the storage tank and the experimental loop was closed. During the subsequent tests, an argon cover gas was maintained above the free surface of ESnBi to minimize oxidation. The electromagnetic pump was then activated to establish steady circulation, and experimental data were recorded under various heating power and flow rate conditions.

where h is the convective heat transfer coefficient, Q is the heating power, D is the outer diameter of the tube, l is the length of the test section, Tiw is the inner wall temperature of the tube, and Tlm is the temperature of ESnBi. To ensure the flow is fully developed, temperature data measured at position 4 (Tiw-4 and Tlm-4 ) are selected for analysis. The heating power (Q) is calculated as:

where Tow is the outer wall temperature of the tube, d is the inner diameter of the tube, δ is the distance between the thermocouple junctions and the inner wall surface of the tube, and k316L is the thermal conductivity of the tube in the test section, which can be calculated using k316L =14.6106+0.01505T [30]. where b is the distance from the measurement point to the inlet of the test section. During post-processing, dimensionless numbers are widely employed to normalize the data and facilitate comparative analysis across different experimental conditions. The dimensionless parameters used in this study are calculated as follows:
(7)

(10)

where Re is the Reynolds number, Pr is the Prandtl number, Pe is the Peclet number, Nu is the Nusselt number, ρ, μ, and k are the density, dynamic viscosity, and thermal conductivity of ESnBi, and v is the velocity of ESnBi.

Obtaining the frictional resistance coefficient of ESnBi is crucial for understanding its flow characteristics and for the accurate design of heat transfer systems in practical applications. The pressure drop obtained from pressure sensors consists of frictional pressure drop, acceleration pressure drop, gravity pressure drop, and form resistance pressure drop, and can be expressed as:
(11)
Δ

Δ
P

+
Δ
P
a c c

P

+
Δ
P
f o r m
In a straight horizontal circular tube, where gravitational effects on ESnBi are negligible and no abrupt expansions or contractions occur, Eq. (11) is accordingly simplified as:

where f is the frictional resistance coefficient, and L is the distance between two pressure sensors. Due to the negligible density variation of ESnBi caused by the temperature difference between pressure sensors, the acceleration pressure drop can be disregarded. Accordingly, the frictional resistance coefficient can be determined using the following equation:
(15)

2

2

To evaluate the accuracy and reliability of the experimental data, uncertainty analysis was performed in accordance with the guide to the expression of uncertainty in measurement (GUM) [46]. According to the GUM framework, uncertainties are categorized into Type A and Type B. Type A uncertainty is assessed through statistical analysis of repeated measurements, and is calculated using the following expression:
(16)
u A
( x )
=
∑
i = 1
n
(
x i
−
x
‾
)
2
n
(
n − 1
)
where xi is the measurement result,
x
‾
is the average of all measurements, and n is the number of measurements. Type B uncertainty is evaluated through non-statistical methods. In this study, it is assumed that Type B uncertainty is attributed exclusively to instrument errors, and the associated measurement errors are uniformly distributed within a known interval. Therefore, Type B uncertainty can be calculated as:
(17)
u
B
( x )
=
σ
i n s
3
where σins is the instrument errors. The ranges and uncertainties of the measuring instruments are listed in Table 4 . And the combined uncertainty of the measured parameters is calculated as follows:
(18)
u
( x )
=
(
u A
( x )

+
(

( x )
)
2

Eq. (19) is used when the indirectly measured quantity is related to directly measured variables through addition or subtraction, while Eq. (20) applies to multiplication and division relationships. Table 5 summarizes the uncertainties associated with all measured variables and indirectly calculated parameters. Fig. 2(a) illustrates the contribution of the uncertainty of each primary variable to the overall uncertainty in the Nusselt number. It can be observed that the temperature difference between the inlet and outlet of the ESnBi, as well as the temperature difference between the tube wall and the ESnBi, are the dominant contributors. This is primarily attributed to the inherently high thermal conductivity of the liquid metal, a phenomenon also noted in Ref.[47]. Fig. 2(b) presents the uncertainty contributions for the frictional resistance coefficient, where the uncertainty in density emerges as the most significant factor.

This study systematically investigates the effects of wall heat flux and Re on the convective heat transfer performance of ESnBi under turbulent flow conditions in a circular tube. The uniform wall heat flux varied from 12.2 kW/m2 to 83.6 kW/m2, and the Re ranged from 6,700 to 23,600. For these operating conditions, axial inner-wall temperature profiles were obtained for different combinations of wall heat flux and Re (Fig. S1), and the corresponding inlet and outlet bulk temperatures of the ESnBi were recorded (Table S1). As shown in Fig. 3(a) , the convective heat transfer coefficient h increases with Re, reflecting the reduction of the thermal boundary layer thickness and the intensification of turbulent mixing near the wall at higher flow velocities, which together enhance convective heat transfer. Furthermore, at a fixed Re, h exhibits a slight increase trend with heat flux, which can be attributed to the temperature dependence of the thermo-physical properties of ESnBi: higher wall and bulk temperatures reduce the viscosity and slightly increase the thermal conductivity, resulting in a modest enhancement of the heat transfer performance. Fig. 3(b) illustrates the variation of the Nu with the Pe, which is calculated using Eq. (9). It is note that the Pr was evaluated from the thermophysical properties of ESnBi at 450 K and treated as a constant in this correlation. The data points are well correlated by Eq. (21), with a maximum deviation of 10 %. As Pe increases, the Nu rises significantly.
(21)
N u = 4.47 + 0.016 P
e
0.78

In the published literature, numerous empirical correlations have been proposed to describe the turbulent convective heat transfer characteristics of liquid metals in circular tubes, most of which can be expressed in the following form [37]:
(22)
N u = a + b P
e
c
P
r
d

Table 6 summarizes the Nu–Pe correlations for various liquid metals reported in the literature, covering media such as Na, NaK, LBE, GaInSn, and Hg. Fig. 4
(a-d) compares the correlation obtained for ESnBi in this study with those of the aforementioned liquid metals over a Pe range of 200–1,000. Fig. 4(a) shows that the present correlation for ESnBi lies within the range of the general liquid metal correlations proposed by Kutateladze et al. [48] and Lubarsky et al. [49], supporting the validity of the current correlation. As shown in Fig. 4(b), ESnBi exhibits a trend comparable to those of LBE and Hg. This similarity between ESnBi and LBE is mainly attributed to their common classification as heavy liquid metals. ESnBi and LBE display broadly similar thermophysical properties, leading to comparable Prandtl numbers. Moreover, the present ESnBi experiments and most LBE studies were performed in austenitic stainless steel circular tubes under an inert gas cover, so that the near-wall roughness, wetting behavior and oxide-layer development are of the same order of magnitude. Notably, the present ESnBi results show excellent agreement with the LBE correlation proposed by Ibragimov et al. [27] within the Pe range of 200-1,000.

In contrast, the Nu–Pe correlations for Na and NaK lie systematically above the present ESnBi correlation (Fig. 4(c)). This can be mainly attributed to their much lower Prandtl numbers (Pr ≈ 0.006), which lead to thinner thermal boundary layers. In addition, alkali liquid metals generally exhibit better wetting behavior on steel surfaces than heavy liquid metals [50,51]. Fig. 4(d) compares ESnBi with the GaInSn correlation proposed by Laube et al. [14], which predicts somewhat higher Nu values. This difference is partly due to the experimental conditions: the GaInSn data were obtained at approximately 25°C in smooth nickel tubes, where corrosion and oxidation effects are strongly reduced and nearly ideal wall conditions can be maintained. The above comparisons demonstrate that turbulent forced convection of liquid metals remains strongly fluid dependent, which underlines the necessity of conducting fluid-specific experimental investigations and developing dedicated correlations for ESnBi.

In view of the similarity in heat transfer behavior between ESnBi and LBE, the experimental data obtained in this study are further compared with previously reported data for LBE (Fig. 5 ). In this figure, Nu is plotted against log10(Pe) to compactly represent the Pe range and facilitate comparison with existing experimental data. The available LBE experimental data are mainly reported by Johnson et al. [56], Ibragimov et al. [27], Zhang et al. [29], and Liu et al. [30]. Among these, the results of Ibragimov et al. [27] and Zhang et al. [29] show the closest agreement with the experimental data obtained in this paper, while those of Johnson et al. [56] and Liu et al. [30] are slightly higher, with a maximum deviation within 35 %. The discrepancies are primarily attributed to differences in experimental conditions and measurement techniques. Previous studies have indicated that gas entrainment in liquid metal flows can reduce the heat transfer coefficient by up to 100 % [57]. In addition, oxide films on the tube walls, surface cleanliness, the oxidation state of the liquid metal, and corrosion behavior may also significantly influence the experimental results.

The relative standard deviation (RSD) was adopted to further evaluate the degree of dispersion between the predicted values obtained by correlations and the experimental data, which is calculated as follows:
(23)
R S
D
N u
=
1
n − 1
∑
i = 1
n
(
N
u
p r e , i
− N
u
exp , i
)
2
N
u
p r e
‾
× 100 %
where Nupre is the predicted Nusselt number obtained from the correlations, Nuexp is the experimental results, and n is the number of data points. Table 7 summarizes the RSD evaluation results of various correlations against the experimental data. The results show that most LBE correlations exhibit considerable deviation when predicting the experimental data reported by Johnson et al. [56]. Excluding the experimental data of Johnson et al. [56], the ESnBi correlation proposed in this study demonstrates reliable predictive accuracy for the Nu of LBE, indicating its broad applicability to heavy liquid metals. For the ESnBi experimental data, the correlations proposed by Ibragimov et al. [27] and Zhang et al. [29] exhibit the best agreement. Fig. 6 compares the predicted values from different correlations with the experimental results, showing that the present correlation (Eq. (21)) and that of Ibragimov et al. [27] exhibits the best agreement with the experimental data for both LBE and ESnBi. By contrast, the correlation proposed by Subbotin et al. [25], Skupinski et al. [26], and Laube et al. [14] consistently overestimates the experimental data, with deviations up to 60 %, 45 %, and 60 %, primarily due to it was developed based on Na, NaK, and GaInSn, whose thermophysical properties differ substantially from those of LBE and ESnBi.

Pressure drop data were obtained under varying flow rates and heat flux conditions (Fig. S2). Using Eq. (15), the relationship between the frictional resistance coefficient f and Re was then determined, as illustrated in Fig. 7 . The results indicate that f gradually decreases with increasing Re. This behavior reflects that f is governed by the structure of the near-wall turbulence. As Re increases at fixed relative roughness, the viscous sublayer becomes thinner and a larger portion of the cross-section is occupied by the logarithmic and outer turbulent regions, which ultimately leads to a reduction in the f. A correlation was obtained by fitting the experimental data, as expressed:
(24)
f =
0.3716
R
e
0.21

The deviation between the fitted correlation and the experimental data remains within 20 %, with larger discrepancies observed at lower Re. This is primarily attributed to the relatively small pressure drop at low flow rates, where minor fluctuations in the measured data may lead to more pronounced effects on the results. According to the definition of the turbulent regime proposed by Nikuradse [58], which was originally derived from water flow experiments and has been widely applied to liquid metal flows as well [37]:
(25)
{
4000 < R e ≤ 26.98
(
d
ε
)
8 7
,
h y d r a u l i c a l l y
s m o o t h
r e g i m e
26.98
(
d
ε
)
8 7
< R e ≤ 4160
(
d
2
ε
)
0.85
,
t r a n s i t i o n a l l y
r o u g h
r e g i m e
R e > 4160
(
d
2
ε
)
0.85
,
f u l l y
r o u g h
r e g i m e
where ε is the absolute roughness. The precision stainless steel tube used in this study was taken to have a roughness of 0.015 mm, in accordance with Ref. [59–61]. In view of the relatively low operating temperatures and the limited duration of the experiments, the wall roughness can reasonably be regarded as constant. Given that the Re ranges from 6,000 to 25,000, the flow is classified as hydraulically smooth regime. Table 8 summarizes the reported f-Re correlations for various liquid metals, and Fig. 8 presents these correlations along with the correlation established in this work. It is evident that substantial discrepancies exist among the f–Re correlations for LBE proposed by different authors. Moreover, although the ESnBi flow in this study lies within the hydraulically smooth regime, its behavior is comparable to that of LBE in the transitionally rough regime. The Blasius equation is generally adopted in engineering practice to estimate f in hydraulically smooth regime [62]:
(26)
f =
0.3164
R
e
0.25

It can be observed from Fig. 8 that all the liquid metal correlations lie significantly above Eq. (26), with the ESnBi correlation reaching approximately 1.7 times higher. These findings indicate the following: (1) The f-Re correlations originally developed for water-based fluids tend to introduce systematic deviations when applied to liquid metals with low Pr, highlighting the need for more targeted correlations. (2) Even under the same flow regimes, discrepancies in experimental conditions across different studies may lead to substantial variations in results, indicating that the f of liquid metals is influenced by multiple interacting factors. (3) Although the ESnBi flow is classified within the hydraulically smooth regime, the presence of oxide inclusions and potential corrosion of the tube may induce a transition toward the transitionally rough regime.

This study systematically investigates the turbulent heat transfer and flow characteristics of ESnBi in a circular tube. However, several limitations should be acknowledged. First, the present study is confined to Pe below 1,000, and experimental data in the high Pe region are lacking. Second, the oxygen content in ESnBi was not strictly controlled during the experiments. Although this condition is more representative of practical applications, it may compromise the accuracy of the experimental results due to oxide inclusions. Third, since no existing studies have yet evaluated the corrosion mechanisms between ESnBi and stainless steel, long-term system operation may potentially intensify corrosion and subsequently influence the heat transfer and flow behavior of ESnBi. These limitations warrant further investigation in future studies.

This study conducted experimental investigations on the flow and heat transfer characteristics of ESnBi in a circular tube under Re ranging from 5,000 to 25,000 and uniform wall heat fluxes from 12.2 kW/m2 to 83.6 kW/m2. Based on the experimental data, a new Nu-Pe correlation for ESnBi was developed: Nu = 4.47+0.016Pe 0.78 (200 < Pe ≤ 1,000). Comparison with correlations for Na, NaK, and LBE reveals that the ESnBi correlation exhibits greater consistency with LBE due to similar thermophysical properties, while those for Na and NaK are higher. RSD analysis indicates that the present correlation provides great predictive accuracy for LBE, with deviations within 25 %, demonstrating broad applicability to heavy liquid metals.

A correlation for the frictional resistance coefficient of ESnBi within the hydraulically smooth regime was developed:
f =
0.3716
R
e
0.21
(5,000 < Re ≤ 25,000). The proposed f–Re correlation is comparable to that of LBE in the transitionally rough regime and is approximately 1.7 times higher than the Blasius equation. The proposed Nu–Pe and f–Re correlations fill a critical gap in the thermal-hydraulic database of Sn–Bi based liquid metals and offer a dedicated basis for design calculations involving ESnBi.

Overall, ESnBi is a lead-free liquid metal with high thermal conductivity, low vapor pressure, and chemical inertness in air, making it a highly promising candidate for medium-to-high temperature thermal management applications. Future research should focus on extending experimental investigations to higher Pe regions, achieving precise oxygen control, and elucidating the corrosion behavior between ESnBi and structural materials. The results of this study provide a strong theoretical foundation for the development and application of ESnBi in advanced thermal systems.

Yuqing Li: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization, Investigation. Guanghao Sang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation. Nan Li: Methodology, Investigation, Visualization. Lei Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Shousheng Tang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Zhongshan Deng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52376082).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2025.128262.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal-hydraulic analysis of the liquid mercury target for the national spallation neutron source

J. Fluids Eng.

Washington: National Advisory Committee for Aeronautics

Nucl. Eng. Des.

Ann. Nucl. Energy
